items.q: What does the fulcrum of a lever represent in the body?
items.correct: The joint, which acts as the pivot point.
items.why: In bodily levers the joint is the fulcrum, muscles provide effort and the body part is the load.

items.q: A biceps curl is an example of which class of lever?
items.correct: A third-class lever, with the effort between the fulcrum and the load.
items.why: Most movements use third-class levers, where the muscle effort lies between joint and load.

items.q: What is the difference between flexion and extension?
items.correct: Flexion decreases the angle at a joint; extension increases it.
items.misconception: They both increase the joint angle.
items.random_wrong: Flexion only happens at the knee.
items.why: Flexion bends a joint (smaller angle) while extension straightens it (larger angle).

items.q: Which lever class describes a biceps curl?
items.why: It's 3rd class. “1st class” is the classic near-miss here; knowing why it's wrong is worth as much as knowing the right answer.

items.q: In which plane does a forward somersault occur?
items.why: Correct answer: Sagittal. “Frontal” sounds right because it borrows the language of the topic — but it fails this question. Re-read the notes on why.

items.q: How many classes of lever are there in the human body?
items.why: Exact answer: 3. Estimation gets you near it; only the anchored number survives the exam hall.
